mappings.rationaleAI: The Atom article directly covers the central subject of Section 111: the nucleus-plus-electron-cloud model (A.1), early particulate theories (A.2), experimental evidence from chemistry and spectroscopy (A.3), and the quantum mechanical model of atomic structure (A.4).

Covers the discovery of the electron as a particle and component of all matter (A.3.c) and its role in the wave-mechanical theory of electronic structure (A.4.c), including orbital probability distributions that determine atomic interactions.

Quantum mechanics underlies the wave-mechanical model of the atom (A.4.c) and provides the theoretical framework for interpreting atomic spectra as quantized electronic transitions (C.2), including the Schrödinger and Heisenberg formulations.

The proton's atomic number Z defines element identity and electron configuration (A.1), and proton count is the basis for atomic weight definitions and isotopic variation (B.1–B.2) that underpin the atomic weight scales of the outline.

Electromagnetic radiation encompasses the atomic spectral emissions central to Section C and includes X-rays as a subrange (D.1), with the article covering photon-based interactions with atomic structure across the full spectrum.

The hydrogen atom spectrum (C.1.a) is the canonical test case for Bohr's theory and wave-mechanical interpretations of quantized transitions (C.2); the article specifically covers quantum mechanical spectral line analysis as a key connection.
The photon is the quantum of electromagnetic radiation emitted in atomic spectral transitions (C.2) and constitutes the X-ray quantum underlying X-ray spectra and bremsstrahlung phenomena (D.1–D.2).

Covers the electron, proton, and nucleus as components of the atom (A.1) and classifies antiparticles (E.1), with wave-particle duality and creation/annihilation events directly relevant to charge conjugation symmetry (E.3).

Particle physics directly addresses antiparticles and antimatter (E.1), the Standard Model's treatment of charge conjugation invariance (E.3), and theoretical speculations about antimatter's cosmic role (E.4).

The Standard Model classifies antiparticles as mirroring particles in mass but opposing charge (E.1) and explicitly addresses matter-antimatter asymmetry and CP-symmetry violation relevant to charge conjugation invariance (E.3).

Traces early philosophical and historical particulate theories of matter from ancient India and Greece (A.2) and covers antimatter and exotic matter forms, directly addressing general properties of antimatter (E.1).

Einstein's 1905 explanation of Brownian motion constitutes direct scientific evidence for the existence of atoms (A.3.f), and his work on mass-energy equivalence and statistical mechanics underpins the fundamental physical constants and their interrelationships (F.1–F.2).

Atomic mass grounds the entire atomic weight section (B.1, B.3), and mass's relativistic relation to energy via E=mc² and its Standard Model origin through the Higgs mechanism are core interrelationships among the physical constants (F.1–F.2).

The discovery of radioactive transformation of one element into another is explicitly listed as category A.3.e of scientific evidence for the nature of atoms, making this article a direct match for that specific outline path.

The periodic table encodes atomic weights organized by atomic number (B.3) and captures isotopic composition and periodic variation (B.1); the article covers atomic weight scales and the history of atomic weight determination that the outline addresses in B.3–B.4.

Chemical elements are defined by atomic number and isotopic composition (B.1–B.2), and characteristic X-ray emission tied to atomic number directly connects elemental identity to X-ray atomic structure analysis (D.2).

Visible light constitutes the observable portion of atomic emission spectra (C.1), and the article's coverage of wave-particle duality and photon detection is directly relevant to spectral line interpretation and X-ray phenomena (D.1).

Electromagnetism governs atomic spectral emission and absorption processes (C.1) and provides the classical framework for X-ray phenomena and bremsstrahlung radiation (D.1–D.2) through Maxwell's equations and QED.

Physical chemistry's use of spectroscopy as an analytical tool directly relates to the spectroscopic evidence for atomic structure (A.3.b) and atomic absorption spectra (C.1.c); the article covers spectroscopy, quantum theory, and electrochemistry as branches.

Mendeleev's formulation of the periodic law required systematic determination and organization of atomic weights, with his corrections to accepted atomic weights representing a direct historical contribution to atomic weight scales (B.3).

Special relativity yields E=mc², a fundamental dimensional interrelationship connecting mass and energy among the physical constants (F.1), and the speed of light c is the defining constant whose value and interrelationships are central to the fundamental constants section (F.2).

Chemical bonds arise directly from the arrangement and behavior of electrons surrounding the nucleus (A.1); the article covers how electron orbital configurations determine all atomic interaction types including covalent, ionic, and metallic bonding.

The neutron is a nuclear component determining atomic mass and isotopic variation (A.1, B.1); neutron count variation defines isotopes, fundamental to atomic weight discussions and the significance of isotopic composition in atomic weight (B.1–B.2).
